pageTitle.value: Spike Lee & Kyle Bell - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: Relating to the experience of Native Americans in his country, Spike Lee chose to mentor Kyle Bell, a young filmmaker from the Thlopthlocco Creek Tribal Town of Oklahoma, who needed to break out of his shell.

WaPageTitle.value: Spike Lee & Kyle Bell - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Rolex mentor Spike Lee has helped his protégé, Native American filmmaker Kyle Bell, break out of his shell and gain work ethic. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Outside the comfort zone - Rolex mentoring programme

text.value: <b>Relating to the experience of Native Americans in his country, Spike Lee chose to mentor Kyle Bell, a young filmmaker from the Thlopthlocco Creek Tribal Town of Oklahoma, who needed to break out of his shell.<b/>

Kyle Bell is a Native American filmmaker from the Thlopthlocco Creek community who grew up in Tulsa, Oklahoma, where he still lives. His documentary films about his people and his tribe have a powerful eloquence, a quiet sense of passion. They seem in stark contrast to the fast-talking, urban-based work of his mentor Spike Lee, director of classic films such as She’s Gotta Have It, Do the Right Thing and Malcolm X, yet through the mentorship the two filmmakers have discovered a lot in common.

“I wanted to work with a Native American filmmaker,” explains Lee. “The original people in this country who have practically been silenced, forgotten, treated horribly in American cinema.”

“I feel a bond with Native Americans. There is definitely a link between them and the Africans, coming over from Africa to this country. And when I met Kyle, I liked his story and his heart.” Bell appreciated the empathy. “He was curious to know about where I come from, and how similar we are. We both come from poor communities.”

During the mentoring period, Bell has watched Lee at work, making a documentary, spending time with him in the editing room. He also appeared in a commercial the director made for cryptocurrency, sitting with him on set – “getting to observe him in the moment, doing what he loves,” Bell says, with a smile. “It was a little taste of what he does, really like magic. To me he is a legend, and to watch and observe and see how he carries himself as a director was amazing. When he talks, every moment is like a teaching moment.”

Lee also invited his protégé to attend the classes he teaches formally at New York University (NYU). “When I was asked to be part of this Rolex mentoring programme, I jumped at the chance,” he says. “I’m an educator who comes from a long line of educators. So automatically I knew that I wanted to mentor a young talent.”

Bell says Lee’s greatest lesson has been in encouraging him to find and follow his voice. He has always wanted to make films, buying a camera and teaching himself to become a filmmaker via YouTube tutorials. “I know what’s within me, but I am very introverted and super shy,” he says. “In film, you have to collaborate and communicate with other people. Spike has really helped me to break out of the shell that perhaps I was hiding in. He’s not shy. He’s the opposite of me because I would rather hide away with the camera and sit back. But he has helped me be vocal about my vision and lead other people along the way.”

title.value: I feel a bond with Native Americans. There is definitely a link between them and the Africans, coming over from Africa to this country. And when I met Kyle, I liked his story and his heart.

text.value:
Lee has been conscious of that role and pushing Bell outside his comfort zone. “I don’t want to hear the word ‘try’,” he says. “You know you got to do it. Words are important and if you keep saying the wrong words, it is going to [have an effect]. You have got to be in that ‘I’m going to do’ mentality versus ‘I’m going to try.’ Big difference.”

Some of his assistance has been intensely practical. For some time, Bell has wanted to make a short film about Jesse Ed Davis, a guitarist from his tribe, who played with John Lennon, George Harrison and Eric Clapton in the 1970s. Lee has helped him with the script.

“The majority of my work is documentary,” Bell says. “But my goal is to break into the narrative world that Spike is part of.” He has already made one fictional short, Spirits, based on people in his life, and plans to make more. “One of the pieces of Spike’s advice was that he doesn’t treat documentaries or narrative films differently. He told me to approach them the same because it’s always about the story and making sure the story connects with people.”

He describes his other “biggest takeaway” as being the need to work hard. “I have gained such a work ethic from Spike,” he says. “He’s really encouraged me to realize that we are in a business where you’ve got to work constantly. You’ve got to stay true to your voice, but also then just keep working on it.”

title.value: In film, you have to collaborate and communicate with other people. Spike has really helped me to break out of the shell that perhaps I was hiding in.

text.value:
Lee is confident that his protégé will have a flourishing career if he follows this advice. “I am going into my fourth decade as a filmmaker and there are still challenges, it’s not just smooth sailing,” he says. “There are always challenges in filmmaking, but it is about how you get past and triumph over those challenges. That’s what it’s about. I hope Kyle gets to make the films he wants to make and tell the story of his people. I am very hopeful about his future.”

Bell has been inspired by the mentorship to do just that. “It has been the most positive thing in the world for me as a director, filmmaker and storyteller to know I have the support and belief of Rolex and Spike... This has been life-changing for me. It is something I am going to carry with me wherever I go, and I will always try to put what I have learned into my work.”
